content:
# Acts Chapter 24 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 255-256 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_024_page_001.jpg, ACT_024_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Five days later, the high priest Ananias came down to Caesarea with some of the elders and a professional attorney named Tertullus. They filed their case against Paul before the governor.

**2** When Paul was brought in, Tertullus opened his prosecution with flattery: "Most Excellent Felix, it is thanks to you that we enjoy a long period of peace, and your foresight has brought reforms to this nation.

**3** We acknowledge this everywhere and in every way with the deepest gratitude.

**4** But not wanting to take up too much of your valuable time, I beg your patience to hear us briefly.

**5** We have found this man to be a plague — a troublemaker who stirs up riots among Jews throughout the entire world and who serves as a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes.

**6** He even tried to desecrate the temple, so we seized him.

**7** [omitted in Sinaiticus]

**8** You yourself, by examining him, will be able to verify everything we are charging him with."

**9** The Jewish leaders joined in the attack, insisting that all of this was true.

**10** Then the governor nodded to Paul, and Paul spoke up: "I know that you have been a judge over this nation for many years, so I am glad to make my defense before you.

**11** You can easily verify that it has been no more than twelve days since I went up to Jerusalem to worship.

**12** They never found me arguing with anyone in the temple, or stirring up a crowd in the synagogues, or anywhere else in the city.

**13** And they cannot prove a single one of the charges they are now bringing against me.

**14** But I will tell you this openly: I do worship the God of our ancestors according to the Way — which they call a 'sect.' I believe everything that is written in the Law and in the Prophets,

**15** and I hold the same hope in God that these men themselves accept — that there will be a resurrection of both the righteous and the unrighteous.

**16** Because of this, I discipline myself to keep a clear conscience before God and before people at all times.

**17** After being away for several years, I came to Jerusalem to bring charitable gifts to my people and to present offerings.

**18** That is what I was doing — ritually purified in the temple, with no crowd and no disturbance — when some Jews from the province of Asia found me.

**19** They are the ones who should be standing here before you, if they have anything against me.

**20** Or else, let these men here state what crime they found when I stood before the Sanhedrin —

**21** unless it is this one thing I shouted out while standing among them: 'It is about the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial before you today!'"

**22** Felix, who actually understood the Way quite well, adjourned the proceedings. "When Commander Lysias comes down," he said, "I will decide your case."

**23** He ordered the centurion to keep Paul in custody but to give him considerable freedom — and to let his friends come and take care of his needs.

**24** Some days later, Felix came with his wife Drusilla, who was Jewish, and sent for Paul. He listened to him talk about faith in Christ Jesus.

**25** But when Paul started discussing justice, self-control, and the coming judgment, Felix grew frightened. "That's enough for now," he said. "You can go. I'll send for you when I find a convenient time."

**26** At the same time, he was hoping Paul would offer him a bribe. So he kept sending for him and talking with him.

**27** Two years went by like this. Then Felix was succeeded by Porcius Festus. And because Felix wanted to do the Jewish leaders a favor, he left Paul in prison.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 2-4 — Tertullus's calculated flattery.** The Greek here drips with political sycophancy. Tertullus calls Felix "most excellent" (kratiste) and credits him with "much peace" (polles eirenes) and "reforms" (katorthomaton). Historically, Felix's administration was violent and corrupt. The irony is thick — this is a hired mouth saying what needs to be said to win a case. I rendered this with the performative register of courtroom flattery to let the insincerity come through.

**Verse 5 — "A plague."** The Greek loimos means literally "a pestilence" — a disease that infects everything it touches. This is character assassination, not legal argument. Tertullus calls Paul a "mover of insurrection" (stasin) among all the Jews "throughout the inhabited world" (oikoumene) — wild prosecutorial exaggeration. "Ringleader" (protostaten) is a military term: the man who stands at the front of a formation. The Nazarene sect designation is meant to reduce the movement to a fringe cult.

**Verses 6-8 — The Sinaiticus text.** Codex Sinaiticus omits what later manuscripts include as verses 6b-8a (the so-called "Western interpolation" about Lysias seizing Paul from the Jews by force). PNEUMA follows the Sinaiticus text here. The transition moves directly from the seizure to the invitation for Felix to examine Paul.

**Verse 14 — "The Way."** Paul reclaims the label. What Tertullus dismisses as a "sect" (hairesin), Paul reframes as "the Way" (hodon) — the living path of faithfulness to the God of Israel. He does not concede an inch. His worship of the ancestral God, his belief in the Law and Prophets — this is not innovation but continuity. Dynamic rendering emphasizes Paul's rhetorical strategy of refusing the prosecution's framing.

**Verse 15 — Resurrection of just and unjust.** Paul strategically aligns himself with the Pharisaic hope, splitting his accusers. The Sadducees denied resurrection; the Pharisees affirmed it. Paul forces the issue into territory where his opponents cannot agree among themselves.

**Verse 22 — Felix's knowledge.** The phrase akribesteron eidos ta peri tes hodou indicates Felix had "more precise knowledge about the Way." This was not ignorance — Felix knew exactly what the movement was. His delay was political calculation, not judicial uncertainty.

**Verse 25 — Felix terrified.** The word emphobos (becoming afraid/terrified) is striking. When Paul spoke of dikaiosynes (justice/righteousness), enkrateias (self-control), and the coming krima (judgment), Felix — a man notorious for corruption and the seduction of Drusilla away from her former husband — heard a personal indictment. His dismissal ("when I find a convenient time") is the classic response of a guilty conscience: not refutation but postponement.

**Verse 26 — The bribe.** Luke's editorial note is devastating in its matter-of-factness. Felix kept summoning Paul not for spiritual hunger but hoping for chremata (money). The governor who was frightened by talk of judgment was simultaneously angling for a bribe. Luke lets the contradiction speak for itself.

**Verse 27 — Political prisoner.** Felix's final act regarding Paul was not justice but political favor-trading. He left Paul bound (dedemenon) as a charis (favor/gift) to the Jewish leadership. Paul became currency in Felix's exit strategy.

-- PNEUMA
